title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: psvr R package implementing four SVR models derived unified mathematical framework percentage-error loss functions. targets forecasting contexts targets yk > 0 strictly positive relative accuracy matters absolute accuracy. Source paper: Unified Family Percentage-Error Support Vector Regression Models Symmetric Kernel Extensions (MDPI Mathematics, 2026). Full derivations paper.tex repository root.

what: Model 1 — ε-SVR with MAPE (Theorem 1 / Appendix A.1)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Primal: Dual QP (max form): βk = αk - αk*, |βk| = αk + αk*. KKT: ω = Σk βk φ(xk), Σk βk = 0, 0 ≤ αk, αk* ≤ 100C/yk. Prediction: f(x) = Σk βk K(xk, x) + b Implementation: QP via osqp. Key files: R/mape_svr.R.
code: min  ½‖ω‖² + C Σk (ξk + ξk*) s.t. (100/yk)(yk - ωᵀφ(xk) - b) ≤ ε + ξk      (100/yk)(ωᵀφ(xk) + b - yk) ≤ ε + ξk*      ξk, ξk* ≥ 0 max  -½ Σk,l βk βl K(xk,xl) + Σk βk yk - (ε/100) Σk |βk| yk s.t. Σk βk = 0      |βk| ≤ 100C / yk    (sample-dependent box; yk small → tight bound)

what: Model 2 — Symmetric ε-SVR with MAPE (Theorem 2 / Appendix A.2)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: primal Model 1, augmented symmetry constraint: ωᵀφ(xk) = · ωᵀφ(-xk) Dual QP (max form): Ks(xi,xj) = K(xi,xj) + ·K(xi,-xj). factor ¼ (vs ½ Model 1) arises symmetrization representer theorem (Appendix .2): ωᵀφ(xk) = ½ Σl βl Ks(xl, xk), quadratic term picks extra ½. Prediction: f(x) = ½ Σk βk Ks(xk, x) + b Implementation: QP via osqp. Build Ks = Ω + ·Ω* inline (½), pass P = ¼·Ks QP quadratic matrix — ¼ factor absorbed P directly. use sym_kernel_matrix() ; helper returns ½(Ω + ·Ω*) = Ωs, correct Model 4. Key files: R/mape_sym_svr.R.
code: max  -¼ Σk,l βk βl Ks(xk,xl) + Σk βk yk - (ε/100) Σk |βk| yk s.t. Σk βk = 0      |βk| ≤ 100C / yk    (unchanged from Model 1)

what: Model 3 — LS-SVR with RMSPE (Theorem 3 / Appendix A.3)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Primal: KKT stationarity: - ω = Σk αk φ(xk) - Σk αk = 0 - ek = (yk²/Γ) αk Linear system (solve directly): YΓ = diag(y1²/Γ, …, yN²/Γ) — added diagonal Ω . Prediction: f(x) = Σk αk K(xk, x) + b Implementation: base::solve() (N+1)×(N+1) augmented matrix. Key files: R/rmspe_lssvr.R.
code: min  ½‖ω‖² + (Γ/2) Σk ek²/yk² s.t. yk = ωᵀφ(xk) + b + ek [ 0    1ᵀ      ] [b]   [0] [ 1    Ω + YΓ  ] [α] = [y]

what: Model 4 — Symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE (Theorem 4 / Appendix A.4)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: primal Model 3, augmented symmetry constraint. Linear system: Ωs = ½(Ω + aΩ*), Ω*kl = K(xk, -xl). Symmetric representer: ωᵀφ(xk) = Σl (Ωs)kl αl Prediction: f(x) = ½ Σk αk Ks(xk, x) + b equivalently: f(x) = Σk αk [K(xk,x) + ·K(xk,-x)] / 2 + b Implementation: base::solve() Ωs replacing Ω. Key files: R/rmspe_sym_lssvr.R.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Required Models 2 4: - K(-xi, xj) = K(xi, -xj) - K(-xi, -xj) = K(xi, xj) RBF kernel K(xi, xj) = exp(-‖xi - xj‖²/2σ²) satisfies conditions: - K(-xi, -xj) = exp(-‖-xi-(-xj)‖²/2σ²) = exp(-‖xi-xj‖²/2σ²) = K(xi,xj) ✓ - K(-xi, xj) = exp(-‖-xi-xj‖²/2σ²) = exp(-‖xi+xj‖²/2σ²) = K(xi,-xj) ✓ Polynomial kernels even degree also satisfy Assumption 3.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: QP solver: osqp package (Models 1 & 2). Supports sparse matrices, commercial license. Linear solver: base::solve() (Models 3 & 4). Dense (N+1)×(N+1) system, extra dep. Target validation: fit() functions call stopifnot((y > 0)). Kernel interface: make_kernel(type, ...) returns closure function(xi, xj) type ∈ {"rbf", "linear", "polynomial"}. Shared across four models. YΓ storage: stored vector y^2 / gamma; added diagonal via diag(Omega) <- diag(Omega) + y_gamma — never materializes full N×N diagonal matrix. "psvr_mape" (Model 1) "psvr_mape_sym" (Model 2) "psvr_rmspe" (Model 3) "psvr_rmspe_sym" (Model 4) S3 methods: predict.psvr_*() class. Documentation: roxygen2 @param, @return, @examples. Style: tidyverse / base R style: snake_case, <- assignment. License: MIT.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: CLAUDE.md — file Package scaffolding: DESCRIPTION, NAMESPACE, directory structure R/kernel.R — make_kernel() helper R/rmspe_lssvr.R — Model 3: rmspe_lssvr() + predict.psvr_rmspe() R/rmspe_sym_lssvr.R — Model 4: rmspe_sym_lssvr() + predict.psvr_rmspe_sym() R/mape_svr.R — Model 1: mape_svr() + predict.psvr_mape() R/mape_sym_svr.R — Model 2: mape_sym_svr() + predict.psvr_mape_sym() tests/ — testthat unit tests four models (84 tests, 0 failures)

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: R 4.5.2 C:\Program Files\R\R-4.5.2\bin\Rscript.exe Run PowerShell: & "C:\Program Files\R\R-4.5.2\bin\Rscript.exe" script.R Always use forward slashes single-quoted strings PowerShell avoid \U Unicode escape errors osqp 1.0.0 uses solve_osqp(P, q, , l, u, pars) directly — osqp(...)$solve() (S7 object, old R6 API gone) Load package R session: devtools::load_all("C:/Users/behep/OneDrive - ITESO/PhD/00-Tesis/psvr")

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: yk > 0 always — validated fit time, never silently coerced. ∈ {-1, 1} symmetric models. symmetric kernel Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + ·K(xi, -xj) uses negation xj, kernel closure must accept negative inputs even training data positive. YΓ diagonal — add diag(Omega) place, never build N×N diagonal matrix. Box constraints Models 1 & 2 per-sample: |βk| ≤ 100C/yk. Prediction Model 2: multiply ½ (symmetric representer theorem). Prediction Model 4: use ½(K(xk,x) + ·K(xk,-x)) per support vector.

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Classical SVR minimises absolute-error losses (MAE, MSE), misaligned scale-free accuracy criteria standard forecasting. error 1 unit negligible target 1 000 large 2. psvr implements four SVR variants derived percentage-error loss functions (Benavides-Herrera et al., 2026): models require strictly positive targets (y > 0), condition percentage residuals well-defined.

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: # CRAN (once released) install.packages("psvr")  # Development version from GitHub remotes::install_github("pbenavidesh/psvr")

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: reproduce experiments reported Tables 1–2 paper using Boston Housing dataset (Harrison & Rubinfeld, 1978). 506 observations strictly positive median home values (MEDV, thousands USD), making MAPE well-defined throughout.
code: library(psvr)  # Boston Housing is available in the MASS package data("Boston", package = "MASS")  # Target: median home value (all > 0) y_all <- Boston$medv X_raw <- as.matrix(Boston[, setdiff(names(Boston), "medv")])  stopifnot(all(y_all > 0)) cat("N =", nrow(X_raw), "  p =", ncol(X_raw),     "  y range: [", round(min(y_all), 1), ",", round(max(y_all), 1), "]\n") #> N = 506   p = 13   y range: [ 5 , 50 ]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Features standardised using training-set statistics RBF kernel operates comparable scale across 13 predictors.
code: set.seed(42) n      <- nrow(X_raw) tr_idx <- sample(n, floor(0.7 * n))  X_raw_tr <- X_raw[tr_idx, ];  y_tr <- y_all[tr_idx] X_raw_te <- X_raw[-tr_idx, ]; y_te <- y_all[-tr_idx]  # Standardise: centre and scale by training mean/sd col_mean <- colMeans(X_raw_tr) col_sd   <- apply(X_raw_tr, 2, sd) X_tr <- scale(X_raw_tr, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd) X_te <- scale(X_raw_te, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd)

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: mape  <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100 rmspe <- function(y, yhat) sqrt(mean(((y - yhat) / y)^2)) * 100 r2    <- function(y, yhat) 1 - sum((y - yhat)^2) / sum((y - mean(y))^2)

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: lm_df_tr <- as.data.frame(X_tr) lm_df_te <- as.data.frame(X_te) lm_fit   <- lm(y_tr ~ ., data = lm_df_tr) lm_pred  <- predict(lm_fit, newdata = lm_df_te)  cat(sprintf("Linear regression — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, lm_pred), rmspe(y_te, lm_pred), r2(y_te, lm_pred))) #> Linear regression — MAPE: 16.29%  RMSPE: 22.48%  R²: 0.7730

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: LS-SVR formulation replaces QP linear system using quadratic penalty percentage residuals. dual reduces : [0𝟏⊤𝟏Ω+YΓ][b𝛂]=[0𝐲] \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \mathbf{1}^\top \\ \mathbf{1} & \Omega + Y_\Gamma \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} b \\ \boldsymbol{\alpha} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \mathbf{y} \end{bmatrix} YΓ=diag(y12/Γ,…,yN2/Γ)Y_\Gamma = \operatorname{diag}(y_1^2/\Gamma, \ldots, y_N^2/\Gamma).
code: K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  fit_ls <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 5000) pred_ls <- predict(fit_ls, X_te)  cat(sprintf("LS-SVR RMSPE  — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, pred_ls), rmspe(y_te, pred_ls), r2(y_te, pred_ls))) #> LS-SVR RMSPE  — MAPE: 15.46%  RMSPE: 27.21%  R²: 0.7277

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: ε-SVR formulation optimises QP sample-dependent box constraints |βk|≤100C/yk|\beta_k| \le 100C/y_k: tighter bounds small targets, concentrating model capacity low-magnitude observations.
code: fit_ep <- mape_svr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 1) pred_ep <- predict(fit_ep, X_te)  cat(sprintf("ε-SVR MAPE    — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, pred_ep), rmspe(y_te, pred_ep), r2(y_te, pred_ep))) #> ε-SVR MAPE    — MAPE: 15.77%  RMSPE: 27.54%  R²: 0.7617 cat(sprintf("Support vectors: %d / %d\n", length(fit_ep$beta), nrow(X_tr))) #> Support vectors: 328 / 354

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Test-set performance Boston Housing (70/30 split, RBF kernel, single run). psvr models improve linear baseline respective percentage-error objectives. LS-SVR formulation (Model 3) minimises RMSPE directly achieves lowest squared-percentage error; ε-SVR formulation (Model 1) targets MAPE sample-dependent box constraints dual variables. results consistent Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026), SVR-MAPE reduces test-set MAPE 15.23% (classical ε-SVR) around 10% relative linear baseline 17.85%.
code: results <- data.frame(   Model = c("Linear regression", "LS-SVR RMSPE (Model 3)",             "\u03b5-SVR MAPE (Model 1)"),   MAPE  = c(mape(y_te,  lm_pred),             mape(y_te,  pred_ls),             mape(y_te,  pred_ep)),   RMSPE = c(rmspe(y_te, lm_pred),             rmspe(y_te, pred_ls),             rmspe(y_te, pred_ep)),   R2    = c(r2(y_te,    lm_pred),             r2(y_te,    pred_ls),             r2(y_te,    pred_ep)) ) results[, 2:4] <- round(results[, 2:4], 2) knitr::kable(results, col.names = c("Model", "MAPE (%)", "RMSPE (%)", "R²"),              align = "lrrr",              caption = paste("Test-set performance on Boston Housing",                              "(70/30 split, RBF kernel, single run)."))

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Harrison, D. & Rubinfeld, D. L. (1978). Hedonic prices demand clean air. Journal Environmental Economics Management, 5(1), 81–102. Benavides-Herrera, P., Álvarez-Álvarez, G., Ruiz-Cruz, R., & Sánchez-Torres, J. D. (2026). unified family percentage-error support vector regression models symmetric kernel extensions. Mathematics, MDPI. (review)

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: vignette verifies R implementations psvr reproduce numerical results reported Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026), originally computed Python using cvxopt (Models 1 & 2) numpy.linalg.lstsq (Models 3 & 4). verification strategy : Use dataset, split seed, hyperparameters Python experiments. Report four metrics: MAPE, RMSPE, R², MSE test set. Compare R outputs paper reference values. Small numerical differences expected acceptable: Python implementation uses cvxopt QP (Models 1 & 2) pseudoinverse via numpy.linalg.lstsq linear system (Models 3 & 4), whereas psvr uses osqp base::solve(), respectively. approaches solve mathematical problem use different numerical algorithms different stopping tolerances, can produce discrepancies order 0.01–0.1 percentage points reported metrics.

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
code: library(psvr)  # Boston Housing — all 506 observations, MEDV strictly positive data("Boston", package = "MASS")  y_all <- Boston$medv X_raw <- as.matrix(Boston[, setdiff(names(Boston), "medv")])  stopifnot(all(y_all > 0)) cat("N =", nrow(X_raw), " p =", ncol(X_raw),     " y range: [", round(min(y_all), 1), ",", round(max(y_all), 1), "]\n") #> N = 506  p = 13  y range: [ 5 , 50 ]

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Python experiments used train_test_split(..., test_size=0.30, random_state=4). replicate set.seed(4) stratified-equivalent random draw.
code: set.seed(4) n      <- nrow(X_raw) tr_idx <- sample(n, floor(0.7 * n))  X_raw_tr <- X_raw[tr_idx, ];  y_tr <- y_all[tr_idx] X_raw_te <- X_raw[-tr_idx, ]; y_te <- y_all[-tr_idx]  cat("Training:", nrow(X_raw_tr), " Test:", nrow(X_raw_te), "\n") #> Training: 354  Test: 152

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Features centred scaled using training-set mean standard deviation, mimicking StandardScaler().fit(X_train).transform(...).
code: col_mean <- colMeans(X_raw_tr) col_sd   <- apply(X_raw_tr, 2, sd)  X_tr <- scale(X_raw_tr, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd) X_te <- scale(X_raw_te, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd)

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
code: mape_fn  <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100 rmspe_fn <- function(y, yhat) sqrt(mean(((y - yhat) / y)^2)) * 100 r2_fn    <- function(y, yhat) 1 - sum((y - yhat)^2) / sum((y - mean(y))^2) mse_fn   <- function(y, yhat) mean((y - yhat)^2)

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Hyperparameters Table 1 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026): Python RBF kernel uses gamma_py convention K(𝐱i,𝐱j)=exp(−γpy∥𝐱i−𝐱j∥2)K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma_{\text{py}} \|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2), equivalent psvr RBF convention K(𝐱i,𝐱j)=exp(−∥𝐱i−𝐱j∥2/2σ2)K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2 / 2\sigma^2) σ=1/(2γpy)\sigma = \sqrt{1 / (2\,\gamma_{\text{py}})}.
code: sigma_m3 <- sqrt(1 / (2 * 0.050967)) cat("Model 3 — sigma:", round(sigma_m3, 6), "\n") #> Model 3 — sigma: 3.132135  K3  <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = sigma_m3) fit_m3 <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K3, gamma = 16962.540770) pred_m3 <- predict(fit_m3, X_te)  m3_mape  <- mape_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_rmspe <- rmspe_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_r2    <- r2_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_mse   <- mse_fn(y_te, pred_m3)  cat(sprintf(   "Model 3 (R) — MAPE: %.4f%%  RMSPE: %.4f%%  R²: %.6f  MSE: %.6f\n",   m3_mape, m3_rmspe, m3_r2, m3_mse )) #> Model 3 (R) — MAPE: 11.0496%  RMSPE: 18.7205%  R²: 0.819582  MSE: 14.127739

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Hyperparameters Table 2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026):
code:
sigma_m1 <- sqrt(1 / (2 * 0.032920)) cat("Model 1 — sigma:", round(sigma_m1, 6), "
") #> Model 1 — sigma: 3.897221  K1  <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = sigma_m1) fit_m1 <- mape_svr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K1, C = 15.631280, eps = 5.804601) pred_m1 <- predict(fit_m1, X_te)  m1_mape  <- mape_fn(y_te, pred_m1) m1_rmspe <- rmspe_fn(y_te, pred_m1) m1_r2    <- r2_fn(y_te, pred_m1) m1_mse   <- mse_fn(y_te, pred_m1)  cat(sprintf(   "Model 1 (R) — MAPE: %.4f%%  RMSPE: %.4f%%  R²: %.6f  MSE: %.6f
",   m1_mape, m1_rmspe, m1_r2, m1_mse )) #> Model 1 (R) — MAPE: 10.6900%  RMSPE: 18.5433%  R²: 0.810768  MSE: 14.817948 cat(sprintf("Support vectors: %d / %d training points
",             sum(fit_m1$beta != 0), nrow(X_tr))) #> Support vectors: 214 / 354 training points

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: table juxtaposes R results computed Python reference values reported Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026). Replace py_* values exact figures paper available final published version. Test-set metrics — Boston Housing, set.seed(4), 70/30 split. Python rows: reference values Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026). NA entries replaced final published figures.
code: # Python reference values from Tables 1-2 of Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) py_m3_mape  <- 10.06        # Table 1, Model 3 — MAPE (%) py_m3_rmspe <- NA_real_     # Table 1, Model 3 — RMSPE (%) not reported py_m3_r2    <- 0.8959       # Table 1, Model 3 — R² py_m3_mse   <- NA_real_     # Table 1, Model 3 — MSE not reported  py_m1_mape  <- 10.29        # Table 2, Model 1 — MAPE (%) py_m1_rmspe <- NA_real_     # Table 2, Model 1 — RMSPE (%) not reported py_m1_r2    <- 0.8602       # Table 2, Model 1 — R² py_m1_mse   <- NA_real_     # Table 2, Model 1 — MSE not reported  cmp <- data.frame(   Model      = c("Model 3 — LS-SVR RMSPE (R)",                  "Model 3 — LS-SVR RMSPE (Python, Table 1)",                  "Model 1 — \u03b5-SVR MAPE (R)",                  "Model 1 — \u03b5-SVR MAPE (Python, Table 2)"),   `MAPE (%)`  = round(c(m3_mape,  py_m3_mape,  m1_mape,  py_m1_mape),  4),   `RMSPE (%)` = round(c(m3_rmspe, py_m3_rmspe, m1_rmspe, py_m1_rmspe), 4),   `R2`        = round(c(m3_r2,    py_m3_r2,    m1_r2,    py_m1_r2),    6),   `MSE`       = round(c(m3_mse,   py_m3_mse,   m1_mse,   py_m1_mse),   6),   check.names = FALSE )  knitr::kable(   cmp,   col.names = c("Implementation", "MAPE (%)", "RMSPE (%)", "R\u00b2", "MSE"),   align     = "lrrrr",   caption   = paste(     "Test-set metrics — Boston Housing, set.seed(4), 70/30 split.",     "Python rows: reference values from Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026).",     "NA entries to be replaced with final published figures."   ) )

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Differences 0.01–0.1 percentage points MAPE/RMSPE R Python implementations normal indicate bug. arise two sources: QP solver (Model 1): osqp uses ADMM-based first-order method, whereas cvxopt uses interior-point method. converge global optimum differ termination tolerances, leading slightly different dual variables hence slightly different predictions. Linear system (Model 3): base::solve() uses LU decomposition partial pivoting, whereas Python implementation uses numpy.linalg.lstsq, applies pseudoinverse via SVD. well-conditioned systems two agree machine precision; ill-conditioned systems (large gamma) small differences can appear. discrepancies exceed 0.5 percentage points, investigate whether random split identical (compare y_te Python test-set targets) whether kernel parameter conversion σ=1/(2γpy)\sigma = \sqrt{1/(2\,\gamma_{\text{py}})} applied correctly.

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Benavides-Herrera, P., Álvarez-Álvarez, G., Ruiz-Cruz, R., & Sánchez-Torres, J. D. (2026). unified family percentage-error support vector regression models symmetric kernel extensions. Mathematics, MDPI. (review) Harrison, D. & Rubinfeld, D. L. (1978). Hedonic prices demand clean air. Journal Environmental Economics Management, 5(1), 81–102.

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: synthetic even-function dataset package README: y=2+x12+0.5x22+εy = 2 + x_1^2 + 0.5\,x_2^2 + \varepsilon, ε∼𝒩(0,0.12)\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,0.1^2). Targets strictly positive construction (y>0y > 0).
code: set.seed(42) n   <- 200 x1  <- runif(n, -3, 3) x2  <- runif(n, -3, 3) y   <- 2 + x1^2 + 0.5 * x2^2 + rnorm(n, sd = 0.1) dat <- data.frame(y = y, x1 = x1, x2 = x2)

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
code: set.seed(1) split <- initial_split(dat, prop = 0.75) train <- training(split) test  <- testing(split)

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: Centre scale predictors RBF kernel operates standardised feature space.
code: rec <- recipe(y ~ x1 + x2, data = train) |>   step_normalize(all_predictors())

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: cost tune() placeholder ; maps Γ\Gamma regularisation parameter inside rmspe_lssvr(). RBF kernel fixed engine argument.
code: K    <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  spec <- psvr_rmspe(cost = tune()) |>   set_engine("psvr", kernel = K)

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
code: wf <- workflow() |>   add_recipe(rec) |>   add_model(spec)

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: search five candidate values cost evaluate fold MAPE. rmspe_lssvr() solves linear system, 25 fits complete seconds. Cross-validated MAPE candidate (lower better):
code: set.seed(2) folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 5)  cost_grid <- tibble::tibble(cost = c(0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100))  tune_res <- tune_grid(   wf,   resamples = folds,   grid      = cost_grid,   metrics   = metric_set(mape) ) collect_metrics(tune_res)[, c("cost", "mean", "std_err")] #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>     cost  mean std_err #>    <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1   0.01 41.8   1.07   #> 2   0.1  40.3   1.08   #> 3   1    30.2   1.10   #> 4  10    12.4   0.398  #> 5 100     4.88  0.0736

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
code: best_params <- select_best(tune_res, metric = "mape") best_params #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>    cost .config         #>   <dbl> <chr>           #> 1   100 pre0_mod5_post0

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: last_fit() refits full training set chosen cost evaluates held-test data. Predictions test set: fitted workflow can also used directly new data:
code: final_wf  <- finalize_workflow(wf, best_params) final_fit <- last_fit(final_wf, split, metrics = metric_set(mape))  collect_metrics(final_fit) #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   .metric .estimator .estimate .config         #>   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl> <chr>           #> 1 mape    standard        5.03 pre0_mod0_post0 preds <- collect_predictions(final_fit) head(preds[, c(".row", "y", ".pred")]) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>    .row     y .pred #>   <int> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1     3  5.99  6.16 #> 2     4  6.20  6.24 #> 3     5  4.69  4.95 #> 4     6  1.96  1.99 #> 5     8  6.70  6.73 #> 6     9  3.93  4.09 new_obs <- data.frame(x1 = c(0, 1, -2), x2 = c(0, 1, 2)) predict(extract_workflow(final_fit), new_data = new_obs) #> # A tibble: 3 × 1 #>   .pred #>   <dbl> #> 1  1.95 #> 2  3.59 #> 3  8.05

title: Authors and Citation
text: Pablo Benavides-Herrera. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Benavides-Herrera P (2026). psvr: Percentage-Error Support Vector Regression. R package version 0.0.0.9000, https://pbenavidesh.github.io/psvr/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {psvr: Percentage-Error Support Vector Regression},   author = {Pablo Benavides-Herrera},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9000},   url = {https://pbenavidesh.github.io/psvr/}, }

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
text: psvr implements four support vector regression models derived unified mathematical framework percentage-error loss functions. Classical SVR minimises absolute-error losses (MAE, MSE), scale-dependent: error 1 unit negligible target 1 000 critical 2. psvr addresses optimising MAPE RMSPE directly, making well-suited forecasting tasks targets strictly positive relative accuracy matters. models require strictly positive targets (y > 0).

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
code: # Development version from GitHub remotes::install_github("pbenavidesh/psvr")

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
text: full worked example Boston Housing dataset — including 70/30 train–test split, feature standardisation, comparison linear baseline — see vignette("getting-started", package = "psvr").
code:
library(psvr)  # Synthetic dataset: even function (f(-x) = f(x)), targets strictly positive set.seed(42) n  <- 100 X  <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) y  <- 2 + X[, 1]^2 + 0.5 * X[, 2]^2 + rnorm(n, sd = 0.1)  tr <- 1:70;  te <- 71:100 X_tr <- X[tr, ];  y_tr <- y[tr] X_te <- X[te, ];  y_te <- y[te]  # Standardise features using training-set statistics (important for RBF) col_mean <- colMeans(X_tr);  col_sd <- apply(X_tr, 2, sd) X_tr_s   <- scale(X_tr, col_mean, col_sd) X_te_s   <- scale(X_te, col_mean, col_sd)  K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  # Model 1 — ε-SVR with MAPE fit1  <- mape_svr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 0.5, eps = 5) pred1 <- predict(fit1, X_te_s)  # Model 2 — Symmetric ε-SVR with MAPE  (a = 1: even-function prior) fit2  <- mape_sym_svr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 0.5, eps = 5, a = 1) pred2 <- predict(fit2, X_te_s)  # Model 3 — LS-SVR with RMSPE fit3  <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 100) pred3 <- predict(fit3, X_te_s)  # Model 4 — Symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE  (a = 1: even-function prior) fit4  <- rmspe_sym_lssvr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 100, a = 1) pred4 <- predict(fit4, X_te_s)  mape <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100  cat(sprintf("Model 1 (MAPE e-SVR):       MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred1))) cat(sprintf("Model 2 (MAPE sym e-SVR):   MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred2))) cat(sprintf("Model 3 (RMSPE LS-SVR):     MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred3))) cat(sprintf("Model 4 (RMSPE sym LS-SVR): MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred4))) #> Model 1 (MAPE e-SVR):       MAPE = 3.59% #> Model 2 (MAPE sym e-SVR):   MAPE = 6.04% #> Model 3 (RMSPE LS-SVR):     MAPE = 3.76% #> Model 4 (RMSPE sym LS-SVR): MAPE = 5.79%

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
text: Benavides-Herrera, P., Álvarez-Álvarez, G., Ruiz-Cruz, R., & Sánchez-Torres, J. D. (2026). unified family percentage-error support vector regression models symmetric kernel extensions. Mathematics, MDPI. (review)

what: Compute a kernel matrix between two sets of points — kernel_matrix
title: Compute a kernel matrix between two sets of points — kernel_matrix
text: Entry [, j] equals K(X1[, ], X2[j, ]). Used internally four model fitting prediction functions.

title: Compute a kernel matrix between two sets of points — kernel_matrix
code: kernel_matrix(K, X1, X2 = X1)

title: Compute a kernel matrix between two sets of points — kernel_matrix
text: K kernel function make_kernel(). X1 Numeric matrix one observation per row (n1 × p). X2 Numeric matrix one observation per row (n2 × p). Defaults X1, giving square training kernel matrix Ω.

title: Compute a kernel matrix between two sets of points — kernel_matrix
text: Numeric matrix size n1 × n2.

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: Returns closure K(xi, xj) suitable use four psvr model fitting functions. returned function accepts numeric vectors sign, required symmetric models (Models 2 4) evaluate K(xk, -xl).

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
code: make_kernel(   type = c("rbf", "linear", "polynomial"),   sigma = 1,   degree = 3L,   coef0 = 1 )

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: type Kernel type: "rbf", "linear", "polynomial". sigma Bandwidth RBF kernel, sigma > 0 (default 1). degree Integer degree polynomial kernel, degree >= 1 (default 3). coef0 Constant term polynomial kernel (default 1).

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: function K(xi, xj) xi xj numeric vectors length, returning scalar kernel evaluation.

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: three supported kernels : RBF: K(xi, xj) = exp(-‖xi - xj‖² / (2 * sigma²)) Linear: K(xi, xj) = xi · xj Polynomial: K(xi, xj) = (xi · xj + coef0)^degree RBF even-degree polynomial kernels satisfy Assumption 3 paper (kernel symmetry), making compatible symmetric models. linear kernel odd-degree polynomial kernels satisfy Assumption 3 used mape_sym_svr() rmspe_sym_lssvr().

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
code: K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 0.5) K(c(1, 2), c(3, 4)) #> [1] 1.125352e-07  K_lin <- make_kernel("linear") K_lin(c(1, 0), c(0, 1)) #> [1] 0  K_poly <- make_kernel("polynomial", degree = 2, coef0 = 1) K_poly(c(1, 2), c(3, 4)) #> [1] 144

what: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
text: Solves quadratic program derived Theorem 1 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) via osqp. dual expressed variables u = [α; α*] ∈ R^{2N} :

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
code: mape_svr(X, y, kernel, C, eps, tol = 1e-05)

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
text: X Numeric matrix training inputs, one observation per row (N × p). y Numeric vector training targets, length N. Must satisfy y > 0. kernel kernel function created make_kernel(). C Regularization parameter C > 0. eps Insensitivity tube half-width ε ≥ 0 (percentage units, .e., tube ε% target). tol Threshold |βk| treated zero selecting support vectors free support vectors (default 1e-5).

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
text: object class "psvr_mape", list components: beta Numeric vector non-zero dual differences βk (length equal number support vectors). b Numeric scalar bias term. X_sv Numeric matrix support vector inputs. kernel kernel function used. eps ε value used.

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
text: P = [Ω, -Ω; -Ω, Ω] (2N × 2N, upper triangular passed osqp) q = [y(ε/100 − 1); y(1 + ε/100)] Equality: [1ᵀ, −1ᵀ] u = 0 Box: 0 ≤ αk ≤ 100C/yk, 0 ≤ αk* ≤ 100C/yk solving, βk = αk − αk* support vectors |βk| > tol retained.

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
code: X <- matrix(1:6, ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- mape_svr(X, y, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 5) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.205 3.990 5.890

what: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: Solves quadratic program derived Theorem 2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) via osqp. symmetry constraint f(x) = ·f(-x) enforced replacing kernel Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + ·K(xi, -xj).

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
code: mape_sym_svr(X, y, kernel, C, eps, a = 1, tol = 1e-05)

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: X Numeric matrix training inputs, one observation per row (N × p). y Numeric vector training targets, length N. Must satisfy y > 0. kernel kernel function created make_kernel(). C Regularization parameter C > 0. eps Insensitivity tube half-width ε ≥ 0 (percentage units). Symmetry parameter: 1 even symmetry f(x) = f(-x), -1 odd symmetry f(x) = -f(-x). tol Threshold |βk| treated zero (default 1e-5).

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: object class "psvr_mape_sym", list components: beta Numeric vector non-zero dual differences βk. b Numeric scalar bias term. X_sv Numeric matrix support vector inputs. kernel kernel function used. eps ε value used. symmetry parameter.

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: dual variables u = [α; α*] ∈ R^{2N} : P = ½ · [Ks, -Ks; -Ks, Ks] osqp's ½ uᵀPu evaluates ¼ βᵀKsβ, matching −¼ coefficient Theorem 2. q = [y(ε/100 − 1); y(1 + ε/100)] (identical Model 1) Equality: [1ᵀ, −1ᵀ] u = 0 Box: 0 ≤ αk ≤ 100C/yk, 0 ≤ αk* ≤ 100C/yk (identical Model 1) Note: Ks = Ω + ·Ω* carries ½ factor . ½ lives P osqp's internal ½ produces required ¼ overall. Contrast rmspe_sym_lssvr() (Model 4), uses sym_kernel_matrix() returning ½(Ω + ·Ω*) = Ωs. kernel must satisfy Assumption 3 paper; see make_kernel().

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
code: X <- matrix(1:6, ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- mape_sym_svr(X, y, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 5, a = 1) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.205 3.990 5.890

title: Predict from a fitted epsilon-SVR with MAPE model — predict.psvr_mape

title: Predict from a fitted epsilon-SVR with MAPE model — predict.psvr_mape
code: # S3 method for class 'psvr_mape' predict(object, newdata, ...)

title: Predict from a fitted epsilon-SVR with MAPE model — predict.psvr_mape
text: object object class "psvr_mape" mape_svr(). newdata Numeric matrix new inputs, one observation per row (M × p). ... Ignored.

title: Predict from a fitted epsilon-SVR with MAPE model — predict.psvr_mape
text: Numeric vector length M predicted values.

title: Predict from a fitted epsilon-SVR with MAPE model — predict.psvr_mape
code: X <- matrix(1:6, ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- mape_svr(X, y, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 5) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.205 3.990 5.890

what: Predict from a fitted symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE model — predict.psvr_mape_sym
title: Predict from a fitted symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE model — predict.psvr_mape_sym
text: Uses symmetric representer theorem: f(x) = ½ Σk βk Ks(xk, x) + b Ks(xk, x) = K(xk, x) + ·K(xk, -x).

title: Predict from a fitted symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE model — predict.psvr_mape_sym
code: # S3 method for class 'psvr_mape_sym' predict(object, newdata, ...)

title: Predict from a fitted symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE model — predict.psvr_mape_sym
text: object object class "psvr_mape_sym" mape_sym_svr(). newdata Numeric matrix new inputs, one observation per row (M × p). ... Ignored.

title: Predict from a fitted symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE model — predict.psvr_mape_sym
text: Numeric vector length M predicted values.

title: Predict from a fitted symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE model — predict.psvr_mape_sym
code: X <- matrix(1:6, ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- mape_sym_svr(X, y, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 5, a = 1) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.205 3.990 5.890

title: Predict from a fitted LS-SVR with RMSPE model — predict.psvr_rmspe

title: Predict from a fitted LS-SVR with RMSPE model — predict.psvr_rmspe
code: # S3 method for class 'psvr_rmspe' predict(object, newdata, ...)

title: Predict from a fitted LS-SVR with RMSPE model — predict.psvr_rmspe
text: object object class "psvr_rmspe" rmspe_lssvr(). newdata Numeric matrix new inputs, one observation per row (M × p). ... Ignored.

title: Predict from a fitted LS-SVR with RMSPE model — predict.psvr_rmspe
text: Numeric vector length M predicted values.

title: Predict from a fitted LS-SVR with RMSPE model — predict.psvr_rmspe
code: X <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6), ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- rmspe_lssvr(X, y, kernel = K, gamma = 1) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.743967 2.904833 2.969809

what: Predict from a fitted symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE model — predict.psvr_rmspe_sym
title: Predict from a fitted symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE model — predict.psvr_rmspe_sym
text: Prediction uses symmetric representer: f(x) = Σₖ αₖ · ½(K(xₖ, x) + ·K(xₖ, -x)) + b.

title: Predict from a fitted symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE model — predict.psvr_rmspe_sym
code: # S3 method for class 'psvr_rmspe_sym' predict(object, newdata, ...)

title: Predict from a fitted symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE model — predict.psvr_rmspe_sym
text: object object class "psvr_rmspe_sym" rmspe_sym_lssvr(). newdata Numeric matrix new inputs, one observation per row (M × p). ... Ignored.

title: Predict from a fitted symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE model — predict.psvr_rmspe_sym
text: Numeric vector length M predicted values.

title: Predict from a fitted symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE model — predict.psvr_rmspe_sym
code: X <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6), ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- rmspe_sym_lssvr(X, y, kernel = K, gamma = 1, a = 1) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.769355 2.853120 2.886170

what: Fit wrappers for parsnip engine dispatch — psvr-fit-wrappers
title: Fit wrappers for parsnip engine dispatch — psvr-fit-wrappers
text: Low-level bridge functions called parsnip fitting psvr model specs. intended direct use; call mape_svr(), mape_sym_svr(), rmspe_lssvr(), rmspe_sym_lssvr() directly instead.

title: Fit wrappers for parsnip engine dispatch — psvr-fit-wrappers
code: psvr_mape_fit(x, y, kernel, C, eps, tol = 1e-05)  psvr_mape_sym_fit(x, y, kernel, C, eps, a = 1L, tol = 1e-05)  psvr_rmspe_fit(x, y, kernel, gamma)  psvr_rmspe_sym_fit(x, y, kernel, gamma, a = 1L)

title: Fit wrappers for parsnip engine dispatch — psvr-fit-wrappers
text: x Numeric predictor matrix (parsnip matrix interface). y Numeric outcome vector. kernel kernel function make_kernel(). C, eps Hyperparameters MAPE models (see mape_svr()). tol Solver zero-threshold (see mape_svr()). Symmetry parameter (1 -1) symmetric models. gamma Regularization parameter RMSPE models (see rmspe_lssvr()).

what: Parsnip model spec: epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — psvr_mape
title: Parsnip model spec: epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — psvr_mape
text: Creates parsnip model specification mape_svr().  kernel solver tolerance engine arguments passed via set_engine().

title: Parsnip model spec: epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — psvr_mape
code: psvr_mape(mode = "regression", engine = "psvr", cost = NULL, svm_margin = NULL)

title: Parsnip model spec: epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — psvr_mape
text: mode "regression" supported. engine "psvr" available. cost Regularization parameter C > 0.  Use hardhat::tune() optimize. svm_margin Epsilon tube half-width ε ≥ 0 (percentage units). Use hardhat::tune() optimize.

title: Parsnip model spec: epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — psvr_mape
text: psvr_mape_model / model_spec object.

title: Parsnip model spec: epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — psvr_mape
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(parsnip) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) spec <- psvr_mape(cost = 10, svm_margin = 5) |>   set_engine("psvr", kernel = K) fit(spec, mpg ~ ., data = mtcars) } # }

what: Parsnip model spec: symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — psvr_mape_sym
title: Parsnip model spec: symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — psvr_mape_sym
text: Creates parsnip model specification mape_sym_svr().  kernel, symmetry parameter , solver tolerance engine arguments.

title: Parsnip model spec: symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — psvr_mape_sym
code: psvr_mape_sym(   mode = "regression",   engine = "psvr",   cost = NULL,   svm_margin = NULL )

title: Parsnip model spec: symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — psvr_mape_sym
text: mode "regression" supported. engine "psvr" available. cost Regularization parameter C > 0. svm_margin Epsilon tube half-width ε ≥ 0 (percentage units).

title: Parsnip model spec: symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — psvr_mape_sym
text: psvr_mape_sym_model / model_spec object.

title: Parsnip model spec: symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — psvr_mape_sym
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(parsnip) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) spec <- psvr_mape_sym(cost = 10, svm_margin = 5) |>   set_engine("psvr", kernel = K, a = 1L) fit(spec, mpg ~ ., data = mtcars) } # }

text: mode "regression" supported. engine "psvr" available. cost Regularization parameter Γ > 0.  Use hardhat::tune() optimize.

title: Parsnip model spec: LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 3) — psvr_rmspe
text: psvr_rmspe_model / model_spec object.

title: Parsnip model spec: LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 3) — psvr_rmspe
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(parsnip) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) spec <- psvr_rmspe(cost = 1) |>   set_engine("psvr", kernel = K) fit(spec, mpg ~ ., data = mtcars) } # }

title: Parsnip model spec: symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 4) — psvr_rmspe_sym
text: Creates parsnip model specification rmspe_sym_lssvr().  kernel symmetry parameter engine arguments.

code: psvr_rmspe_sym(mode = "regression", engine = "psvr", cost = NULL)

text: mode "regression" supported. engine "psvr" available. cost Regularization parameter Γ > 0.

title: Parsnip model spec: symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 4) — psvr_rmspe_sym
text: psvr_rmspe_sym_model / model_spec object.

title: Parsnip model spec: symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 4) — psvr_rmspe_sym
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(parsnip) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) spec <- psvr_rmspe_sym(cost = 1) |>   set_engine("psvr", kernel = K, a = 1L) fit(spec, mpg ~ ., data = mtcars) } # }

what: Fit LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 3) — rmspe_lssvr
title: Fit LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 3) — rmspe_lssvr
text: Solves linear system derived Theorem 3 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026). primal objective ½‖ω‖² + (Γ/2) Σ eₖ²/yₖ², leading (N+1)×(N+1) system

title: Fit LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 3) — rmspe_lssvr
code: rmspe_lssvr(X, y, kernel, gamma)

title: Fit LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 3) — rmspe_lssvr
text: X Numeric matrix training inputs, one observation per row (N × p). y Numeric vector training targets, length N. Must satisfy y > 0. kernel kernel function created make_kernel(). gamma Regularization parameter Γ > 0.

title: Fit LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 3) — rmspe_lssvr
text: object class "psvr_rmspe", list components: alpha Numeric vector dual variables (length N). b Numeric scalar bias term. X_train training matrix X (kept prediction). kernel kernel function used.

title: Fit LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 3) — rmspe_lssvr
text: YΓ = diag(y₁²/Γ, …, yN²/Γ) added diagonal Ω.

title: Fit LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 3) — rmspe_lssvr
code: X <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6), ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- rmspe_lssvr(X, y, kernel = K, gamma = 1) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.743967 2.904833 2.969809

what: Fit symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 4) — rmspe_sym_lssvr
title: Fit symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 4) — rmspe_sym_lssvr
text: Solves linear system derived Theorem 4 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026). Identical structure rmspe_lssvr() (Model 3) replaces kernel matrix Ω symmetrized matrix Ωs = ½(Ω + ·Ω*), Ω*ₖₗ = K(xₖ, -xₗ). system solved

title: Fit symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 4) — rmspe_sym_lssvr
code: rmspe_sym_lssvr(X, y, kernel, gamma, a = 1)

title: Fit symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 4) — rmspe_sym_lssvr
text: X Numeric matrix training inputs, one observation per row (N × p). y Numeric vector training targets, length N. Must satisfy y > 0. kernel kernel function created make_kernel(). gamma Regularization parameter Γ > 0. Symmetry parameter: 1 even symmetry f(x) = f(-x), -1 odd symmetry f(x) = -f(-x).

title: Fit symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 4) — rmspe_sym_lssvr
text: object class "psvr_rmspe_sym", list components: alpha Numeric vector dual variables (length N). b Numeric scalar bias term. X_train training matrix X (kept prediction). kernel kernel function used. symmetry parameter.

title: Fit symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 4) — rmspe_sym_lssvr
text: YΓ = diag(y₁²/Γ, …, yN²/Γ). kernel must satisfy Assumption 3 paper (kernel symmetry): K(-xi, xj) = K(xi, -xj) K(-xi, -xj) = K(xi, xj). RBF even-degree polynomial kernels satisfy ; see make_kernel().

title: Fit symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 4) — rmspe_sym_lssvr
code: X <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6), ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- rmspe_sym_lssvr(X, y, kernel = K, gamma = 1, a = 1) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.769355 2.853120 2.886170

what: Compute the symmetrized kernel matrix Ωs = ½(Ω + a·Ω*) — sym_kernel_matrix
title: Compute the symmetrized kernel matrix Ωs = ½(Ω + a·Ω*) — sym_kernel_matrix
text: Used symmetric LS-SVR model (Model 4). Entry [k, l] Ω* K(xk, -xl), negation applied columns X (.e., X2).

title: Compute the symmetrized kernel matrix Ωs = ½(Ω + a·Ω*) — sym_kernel_matrix

title: Compute the symmetrized kernel matrix Ωs = ½(Ω + a·Ω*) — sym_kernel_matrix
text: Numeric N × N matrix Ωs = ½(Ω + ·Ω*).

what: Compute a symmetric kernel vector for prediction — sym_kernel_vector
title: Compute a symmetric kernel vector for prediction — sym_kernel_vector
text: new point x, returns N-vector entry k equal ½ * Ks(X[k, ], x) Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + * K(xi, -xj). Used predict.psvr_mape_sym() predict.psvr_rmspe_sym().

title: Compute a symmetric kernel vector for prediction — sym_kernel_vector
text: K kernel function make_kernel(). X Numeric training matrix (N × p). x Numeric vector (length p), new point predict. Symmetry parameter: 1 (even) -1 (odd).

title: Compute a symmetric kernel vector for prediction — sym_kernel_vector
text: Numeric vector length N.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: Initial development release.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: mape_svr() — epsilon-SVR MAPE loss (Model 1). Solves dual QP via osqp per-sample box constraints |βk| ≤ 100C/yₖ. mape_sym_svr() — symmetric epsilon-SVR MAPE loss (Model 2). Enforces f(x) = ·f(-x) replacing kernel symmetric kernel Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + ·K(xi, -xj). rmspe_lssvr() — LS-SVR RMSPE loss (Model 3). Solves (N+1)×(N+1) linear system directly via base::solve(). rmspe_sym_lssvr() — symmetric LS-SVR RMSPE loss (Model 4). linear system Model 3 symmetrized kernel matrix Ωs = ½(Ω + ·Ω*). four models derived unified percentage-error loss framework; see Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) mathematical proofs.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: make_kernel() — factory returning kernel closure type ∈ {"rbf", "linear", "polynomial"}. RBF even-degree polynomial kernels satisfy symmetry assumption (Assumption 3) required Models 2 4.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: Four parsnip model specifications use within tidymodels workflows: psvr_mape() — spec Model 1; hyperparameters cost svm_margin. psvr_mape_sym() — spec Model 2; hyperparameters cost svm_margin. psvr_rmspe() — spec Model 3; hyperparameter cost (maps Γ). psvr_rmspe_sym() — spec Model 4; hyperparameter cost (maps Γ). kernel (symmetric models) symmetry parameter engine arguments supplied via set_engine("psvr", kernel = ..., = ...). Hyperparameters map dials::cost() dials::svm_margin() compatibility tune_grid().

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: 84 unit tests covering four models: kernel correctness, QP/linear-system solutions, support vector selection, bias recovery, predict consistency, input validation.
